figures.caption: FIG. 4: Chiral-molecule-induced modification of skyrmion textures. Stray magnetic field images of skyrmions in bare, D-AHPA, and L-AHPA functionalized regions measured under Bz = ±1.6mT following the application of a ∼ 30mT IP magnetic pulse. While isolated skyrmions dominate the observed textures, a small fraction of residual labyrinthine domains persists. The lower panels show the corresponding skyrmion density within the FOV.

FIG. 5: Statistical distributions of relative changes: in skyrmion diameter, dSk−Sk, and shape factor for D- and L-AHPA functionalized regions relative to the non-functionalized regions under Bz = ±1.6mT.

FIG. 8: Wide-field NV magnetometry setup. (a) Schematic of the optical and microwave setup. (b) Photographs of the experimental setup showing the sample holder with microwave loop and the optical table configuration.

FIG. 1: (a) Sample design: Schematic of the multilayer magnetic stack with a ∼3 nm Au capping layer, where chiral molecules are deposited on only half of the surface, leaving an adjacent non-functionalized region for direct comparison. Molecular characterization: (b) Circular dichroism (CD) spectra of D- and L-AHPA solutions measured in the UV range (210–280 nm), showing mirror-symmetric extrema near ∼240–245 nm that confirm opposite molecular handedness; the ethanol reference remains close to zero. (c) Contact angle measurements for bare and molecule-functionalized regions, demonstrating the change in surface wettability after adsorption of D-AHPA and L-AHPA molecules. (d) NV magnetometry workflow: A sequence of photoluminescence frames acquired during a microwave frequency sweep yields pixel-resolved ODMR spectra for the FOV, each spectrum is fitted with a lorentzian function to determine the resonance positions and splitting, enabling quantitative reconstruction of the stray magnetic field map.

FIG. 2: Evolution of magnetic domains to skyrmion phase in CoFeB thin film under increasing Bz field. Background-subtracted NV magnetic field maps (∆BNV) reveal the progressive deformation and coalescence of skyrmions together with stripe domains by increasing OOP field. The magnetic contrast is normalized by the standard deviation σ (∼ 1.15µT) of the field distribution, so that the color scale reflects statistically significant deviations from the background. The faint concentric ring pattern originates from optical interference caused by reflections within the diamond.

FIG. 7: (a) Energy-level diagram of the NV − center. The NV − center has a spin-1 ground-state triplet, 3A2; within this manifold the mS = 0 and mS = ±1 sublevels are split by the zero-field splitting D ≈ 2.87GHz at room temperature (this splitting is internal to the ground triplet). The center also has an excited triplet state, 3E; optical transitions between 3A2 and 3E (zero-phonon line at ∼ 637 nm) are used for optical excitation and readout. Continuous-wave 532 nm green laser excitation pumps population from 3A2 to 3E. Relaxation back to the ground state occurs either via radiative (optically allowed) decay or via spin-selective non-radiative decay through intermediate singlet states, which gives rise to spin-dependent fluorescence contrast. Dashed arrows indicate non-radiative or spin-non-conserving pathways. Spin sublevels are labeled by their magnetic quantum numbers (mS = 0,±1). MW control enables coherent spin manipulation between these sublevels, and an external magnetic field causes Zeeman splitting of the mS = ±1 sublevels. (b) {100}-cut diamond lattice. The NV center direction (orange arrow) along ⟨111⟩ and the applied magnetic field Bz (surface normal) [53]. (c) ODMR spectra of the NV center; the upper plot shows the resonance at zero magnetic field (B = 0). The lower plot shows the Zeeman splitting under a finite magnetic field (B > 0), where the resonance splits into two peaks separated by 2γBNV , where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio (28.024GHz/T).

FIG. 3: Magnetic field profiles of DWs and skyrmions. (a, b) Insets display magnified regions; the white line in (a) indicates the path used to extract the line profile across a DW, while the dashed circle in (b) marks a representative skyrmion used for analysis. (c, d) Corresponding stray magnetic field BNV as a function of lateral displacement across a DW (c) and a skyrmion (d), centered at 0. Dashed lines represent Gaussian fits used to extract the full width at half maximum (FWHM).
